html:
Bunker Hill Mining (BHLL): A Messy Capital Stack Hiding a Near-Term U.S. Silver-Base Metals Restart

Bunker Hill is the kind of stock the market usually prices badly: no trailing revenue, an ugly liability table, a recent 1-for-35 reverse split, dilution chatter, and a mine restart that is still not producing. That surface picture is real, but it is also why the opportunity exists. Underneath it is a fully built, near-commissioning U.S. zinc-silver-lead mine whose economic exposure is far better than the stale 2022 model suggests.

The old PFS was built around roughly $21.70/oz silver, $1.29/lb zinc, and $0.95/lb lead, producing about $41 million of average annual EBITDA and $28 million of average annual free cash flow. Today silver is roughly $76/oz, zinc is around the mid-$1.40s/lb, and lead is near the PFS deck. Applying current-cycle prices to the same mine plan turns Bunker Hill from a marginal restart into a high-torque cash-flow asset. The silver line alone can add more than $30 million of annual revenue versus the PFS deck before normal adjustments. That is the crux: the market is still debating whether the old mine works, while the commodity deck has already made the prize much larger.

The company is now very close to the point where the valuation framework changes. Bunker Hill has repeatedly said the mine is on track for a June 2026 restart, with construction and commissioning advancing through the spring, the mills turning, the filter plant installation progressing, and underground stockpiles sufficient for the first three months of operations. The March 31, 2026 10-Q shows $30.5 million of cash, $36.5 million of current assets, $12.7 million of working capital, and management’s statement that it has enough working capital for the next 12 months. Shares outstanding were 46.7 million as of May 6, 2026. Current BHLL price is about $4.29, implying a roughly $200 million market cap.

The best part of the thesis is that the old bear case is becoming stale. The prior Sprott stream that took 10% of payable metals was terminated in June 2025 and exchanged for equity, a convertible debenture, and an additional 1.65% life-of-mine gross revenue royalty. That is still expensive capital, but it removes a major early-production drag that many investors still appear to assume exists. The company still carries a large silver loan and secured debt, so this is not clean, but the economics are no longer capped in the same way.

The second variant perception is the silver mix. Bunker Hill is often screened as a zinc restart, but management is explicitly pushing the asset back toward a 50/50 silver/base-metals revenue mix. That matters because the market pays more for silver exposure than for zinc exposure, especially in the current silver tape. The latest Cate-Eight drilling supports the concept: BHE26-02 hit 28.6 feet grading 3.02 oz/t silver, 7.44% lead, and 0.85% zinc, including narrow zones over 20% lead and nearly 9 oz/t silver, within 40 feet of existing underground development. Early holes do not make a mine, but near-infrastructure high-grade silver is exactly the kind of result that can change short-cycle mine sequencing and market perception.

The third variant perception is expansion. The corporate presentation points to a Bunker Hill 2.0 scenario using existing mineral inventory: 2,500 tpd versus 1,800 tpd, 13-year mine life, 920,000 oz of annual silver production, 54% higher silver output, and 22% lower AISC. The company also references a $150 million U.S. EXIM Bank LOI. None of that should be capitalized as a certainty today, but it gives the equity a second-stage option: first re-rate from developer to producer, then re-rate from small restart to longer-life domestic critical-metals platform.

The market’s skepticism is understandable. The 10-Q still shows no revenue, a $84.6 million silver loan fair value, $14.6 million debt facility, $16.2 million of convertible debenture carrying value, EPA obligations, and 17.9 million warrants outstanding. The $45.1 million warrant liability is noncash under U.S. GAAP, but the dilution is real if the stock works. Retail context is also telling: Reddit and filing-bot chatter is focused more on dilution risk than on production upside, while Stocktwits-style sentiment is thin and stale. This is not a crowded retail squeeze trade. It is an ignored, complex mining transition with a credibility discount.

That discount should narrow quickly if June restart happens and first concentrate ships. At that point, investors can stop valuing BHLL as a binary developer and start valuing it on current-cycle EBITDA. On my base case, 1,800 tpd steady-state production at current-cycle but not peak silver pricing can support roughly $75 to $85 million of annual EBITDA before full expansion. A 5.0x to 5.5x producer multiple supports $375 to $465 million of enterprise value, with dilution partly offset by warrant exercise cash and debt conversion.

The downside is not abstract. Brownfield restarts break schedules. Paste backfill, tailings filtration, underground ground control, metallurgical recoveries, water treatment, and smelter terms all have to work together. A six-month delay plus a weak silver tape would likely force the market back to dilution math. The Crescent litigation, Superfund history, and ongoing IDEQ water treatment costs also keep this from being a clean U.S. mining story.

Still, the asymmetry is attractive because the critical test is now measured in weeks, not years. The stock is priced like a messy junior developer. If the mine restarts and ramps, it becomes a small U.S. producer with meaningful silver torque, existing infrastructure, a Teck/Sprott-backed capital base, and near-mine high-grade exploration that can matter fast.

Bear case: $2.25, down 48%. Restart slips, silver falls, capital needs return, and the stock trades as a diluted option on a troubled asset.

Base case: $7.25, up 69%. June restart occurs, ramp-up reaches credible 1,800 tpd trajectory, EBITDA power becomes visible, and BHLL trades around 5x current-cycle forward EBITDA after dilution.

Bull case: $12.50, up 191%. Silver stays elevated, Cate-Eight and Ranger-Page validate a higher-silver mine plan, Bunker Hill 2.0 financing becomes credible, and the market values the company as a domestic silver-base-metals growth producer rather than a restart gamble.
